request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"friendster::social_communication","name":"Friendster","one_liner":"One of the original social networking platforms that pioneered online friend graphs before being overtaken by MySpace and Facebook, eventually pivoting to social gaming in Southeast Asia before shutting down in 2015.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are ad-supported B2C social platforms targeting consumers at no direct cost, relying on ad revenue (per Trusted facts: monetization=ad_revenue). However, Friendster was a broad-based text/photo/video social network, whereas the pitch is a narrow-format voice-note app with a location constraint — a meaningfully different content mechanic even if the revenue model label matches."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both compete in the B2C social networking space for consumer engagement and attention. Friendster started US-focused and shifted to Southeast Asia. The new pitch targets college campuses ('college towers'), a hyper-local sub-segment of the same broad consumer social market. Same macro sector, very different demographic and geographic focus."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Friendster grew through broad web-based word-of-mouth and press coverage (Time, Vanity Fair, etc.), not a focused viral-on-campus GTM. The new pitch explicitly names college towers as a viral wedge — a classic campus-first playbook (closer to Facebook's early GTM) that Friendster never employed. Both rely on network effects but through very different ignition strategies."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Friendster launched in 2002, reached 1M users within months, turned down a $30M Google acquisition at a $53M valuation, and raised tens of millions from top-tier VCs. The new pitch reads as an early-stage idea with no disclosed funding or traction, operating at a fraction of that scale. The stage gap is large."}},"why_similar":"Both are ad-supported, consumer-facing social platforms that depend on user-generated content, network effects, and ad revenue as the primary monetization lever. Both operate (or aim to operate) globally, targeting the same broad customer type (consumer) in the social-communication sector. Friendster's early viral growth among young adults mirrors the new pitch's aspiration to go viral in college communities.","where_diverged":"1. Content format: The new pitch constrains posts to 30-second voice notes — an audio-first, ephemeral-style format — whereas Friendster was text, photo, and video-centric. 2. Location mechanic: Posts are tied to a single recurring physical location, creating hyper-local identity, which Friendster never attempted. 3. GTM wedge: The new pitch targets college dormitory towers as a deliberate viral nucleus; Friendster had no campus-first strategy and grew through broad press coverage. 4. Scale/stage: Friendster was a VC-backed platform that turned down $30M acquisition offers; the new pitch is pre-scale. 5. Geography focus: Friendster ultimately concentrated in Southeast Asia; the new pitch's viral signal is US college campuses.","failure_causes":["outcompeted by Facebook and MySpace on product velocity and network density","chronic platform performance and scalability problems alienating early users","revolving-door leadership instability (five CEOs in roughly four years)","declined $30M Google acquisition — valuation later collapsed to <5% of 2003 level","failed to retain Western user base while Southeast Asia pivot came too late","pivot to social gaming diluted brand identity and shed existing social graph","lack of sustained user engagement leading to irreversible network-effect collapse"],"lessons_for_input":["Prioritize platform performance and reliability before scaling — Friendster lost its early adopters primarily due to slow load times and bugs, not concept failure.","Define and defend a focused geographic or demographic beachhead (e.g., one campus network) before expanding; Friendster tried to be global too fast and lost depth everywhere.","Seriously evaluate early acquisition or partnership offers against your ability to outbuild well-funded rivals — declining Google's $30M offer proved catastrophic.","Keep leadership stable through growth phases; constant CEO turnover at Friendster destroyed strategic continuity at critical inflection points.","A novel content format (voice notes + location) can be a durable differentiator only if you build community rituals around it — avoid pivoting away from your core mechanic the moment growth stalls."]}
